symptoms.symptom_name: Multiple long-standing 'stuck-on' pigmented truncal lesions increasing with age

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Multiplicity of stuck-on pigmented lesions increasing with age is the hallmark demographic pattern of seborrhoeic keratoses.
Melanoma is typically a single evolving lesion, not one of many stable identical spots.
The 'stuck-on', sharply demarcated, verrucous appearance is characteristic of seborrhoeic keratosis.
BCC is typically pearly/translucent with a rolled border, not warty and crumbly.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bleeding triggered by mechanical avulsion (lesion caught on towel), not spontaneous

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A superficial exophytic benign lesion is easily caught and avulsed; a clear mechanical trigger explains the bleed.
Mechanical avulsion accounts for the bleeding, in contrast to the spontaneous ulceration/bleeding of an invasive tumour.

symptoms.symptom_name: Darkly pigmented (dark brown to black) lesion that has bled

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dark pigmentation plus bleeding mandates active exclusion of melanoma regardless of the otherwise benign story — the driver of the in-person referral.
Seborrhoeic keratoses are commonly heavily pigmented, so dark colour alone does not discriminate.

symptoms.symptom_name: No spontaneous change in size, shape or colour prior to trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of the ABCDE evolution features lowers melanoma probability, though it cannot exclude it without dermoscopy.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dermoscopy: multiple pseudohorn cysts (keratin plugs), sharp demarcation; no atypical pigment network, blue-white veil, or arborising vessels

investigation_details.expected_result: Multiple pseudohorn cysts and cerebriform/comedo-like openings with a sharply demarcated stuck-on pattern; absence of an atypical pigment network, blue-white veil, or arborising vessels — features diagnostic of a benign keratinocytic lesion and against melanoma.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without dermoscopy the pigmented lesion cannot be confidently characterised and melanoma cannot be formally excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pseudohorn cysts with a stuck-on pattern and no melanoma criteria are essentially diagnostic of seborrhoeic keratosis.
Absence of atypical network, blue-white veil and arborising vessels argues strongly against melanoma and pigmented BCC.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpable regional lymphadenopathy and no deep induration

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of nodes and induration is reassuring against invasive/metastatic disease but is a clinician examination finding, unavailable via chat.
Aspirin does not bear on the lesion diagnosis but explains the exaggerated, prolonged bleeding from a minor avulsion.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Multiplicity of stuck-on lesions + a warty sharply-demarcated surface + dermoscopic pseudohorn cysts together constitute an essentially diagnostic benign keratinocytic constellation, which is why a competent assessor is confident in the benign impression even while arranging confirmatory in-person review.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: In-person dermoscopy is the appropriate first-line assessment of a pigmented lesion — confirms the benign keratinocytic features and formally excludes melanoma; cannot be performed over telehealth chat.
Reserved for any diagnostic doubt on dermoscopy — definitive to exclude melanoma. Not required if dermoscopy is clearly benign.
No role in the assessment of a localised benign-appearing pigmented lesion — ordering these would be over-investigation.
